perturbation.original_text: Neither a Disclosing Party nor any of its Representatives has made or is making any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of any of such Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, and neither such Disclosing Party nor any of its Representatives will have any liability to the Recipient or to any of the Recipient’s Representatives relating to or resulting from the use of any of such Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information or any inaccuracies or errors therein or omissions therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither a Disclosing Party nor any of its Representatives has made or is making any representation or warranty as to the accuracy of any of such Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, and neither such Disclosing Party nor any of its Representatives will have any liability to the Recipient or to any of the Recipient’s Representatives relating to or resulting from the use of any of such Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'express or implied', 'or completeness' and 'or any inaccuracies or errors therein or omissions therefrom' the provision is changed to limit liability for only accuracy and not completeness. While Delaware permits parties to limit liability, a blanket disclaimer without regard to conduct could be problematic depending on the nature of the information and the conduct of the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware contract law limitations on liability clauses
perturbation.law_citation: Enforceability of Limitation of Liability Clauses in Delaware
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law generally enforces limitation of liability clauses. This altered clause could potentially be interpreted as an overbroad attempt to disclaim liability, conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, and potentially making it unenforceable depending on the circumstances surrounding the disclosure of information.

Laws, c. 349. ;Â Â§ 2-702. Sellerâs remedies on discovery of buyerâs insolvency. (1) Where the seller discovers the buyer to be insolvent he or she may refuse delivery except for cash including payment for all goods theretofore delivered under the contract, and stop delivery under this Article (Section 2-705). (2) Where the seller discovers that the buyer has received goods on credit while insolvent he or she may reclaim the goods upon demand made within ten days after the receipt, but if misrepresentation of solvency has been made to the particular seller in writing within three months before delivery the ten day limitation does not apply. Except as provided in this subsection the seller may not base a right to reclaim goods on the buyerâs fraudulent or innocent misrepresentation of solvency or of intent to pay. (3) The sellerâs right to reclaim under subsection (2) is subject to the rights of a buyer in ordinary course or other good faith purchaser or lien creditor under this Article (Section 2-403). Successful reclamation of goods excludes all other remedies with respect to them. 5A Del. C. 1953, Â§Â§ â2-702;Â 55 Del. Laws, c. 349 ;Â 70 Del. Laws, c. 186, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 2-703. Sellerâs remedies in general. Where the buyer wrongfully rejects or revokes acceptance of goods or fails to make a payment due on or before delivery or repudiates with respect to a part or the whole, then with respect to any goods directly affected and, if the breach is of the whole contract (Section 2-612), then also with respect to the whole undelivered balance, the aggrieved seller may (a) withhold delivery of such goods; (b) stop delivery by any bailee as hereafter provided (Section 2-705); (c) proceed under the next section respecting goods still unidentified to the contract; (d) resell and recover damages as hereafter provided (Section 2-706); (e) recover damages for non-acceptance (Section 2-708) or in a proper case the price (Section 2-709); (f) cancel. 5A Del. (1) An aggrieved seller under the preceding section may (a) identify to the contract conforming goods not already identified if at the time he or she learned of the breach they are in his or her possession or control; (b) treat as the subject of resale goods which have demonstrably been intended for the particular contract even though those goods are unfinished. (2) Where the goods are unfinished an aggrieved seller may in the exercise of reasonable commercial judgment for the purposes of avoiding loss and of effective realization either complete the manufacture and wholly identify the goods to the contract or cease manufacture and resale for scrap or salvage value or proceed in any other reasonable manner. 5A Del. perturbation.justification: The original text disclaims liability for inaccuracies, errors, and omissions in the disclosed confidential information, while the changed text removes the disclaimer for 'completeness' and 'omissions'. The Law Explanation suggests this could conflict with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, especially if the omission is significant. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that removing liability for omissions could be problematic. Scraped Law Snippet 1, while related to sales, shows the importance of remedies and obligations within contracts under Delaware law. Although the snippet doesn't directly address limitation of liability clauses, it sets a precedent of remedies if a contract is breached. The altered text removes a disclaimer, which changes the legal obligations of the parties. Therefore, the modified text introduces a legal contradiction by removing liability protection for important factors, potentially violating an implied duty.

perturbation.original_text: During the nine (9) month period commencing on the Effective Date, neither Party nor any of such Party’s Representatives will directly or indirectly solicit for employment, hire or induce or encourage (in each case, other than by means of a general solicitation pursuant to a newspaper or other media advertisement or other customary means by such party in the ordinary course of its business) any Covered Person (as defined herein) to terminate his or her relationship with the other Party or any subsidiary or other affiliate of said other Party in order to become an employee, consultant or independent contractor of or to any other Person.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Party nor any of such Party’s Representatives will directly solicit for employment any Covered Person (as defined herein) to terminate his or her relationship with the other Party or any subsidiary or other affiliate of said other Party in order to become an employee, consultant or independent contractor of or to any other Person.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'indirectly', 'hire or induce or encourage', and '(in each case, other than by means of a general solicitation pursuant to a newspaper or other media advertisement or other customary means by such party in the ordinary course of its business)', the prohibition on soliciting employees is significantly weakened. This could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially if it prevents employees from seeking better opportunities, thus undermining the Delaware law on competition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding non-solicitation agreements and unreasonable restraints on trade
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law scrutinizes non-solicitation agreements to ensure they are reasonable in scope and duration and do not unduly restrict employee mobility. The weakened non-solicitation clause could be argued as an unreasonable restraint if its application is too broad or indefinite, conflicting with Delaware's policy favoring employee freedom.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 2 Authenticated PDF Sales Part 1. Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter Part 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract Part 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract Part 4. Title, Creditors and Good Faith Purchases Part 5. Performance Part 6. Breach, Repudiation and Excuse Part 7. Remedies
perturbation.justification: The original text placed restrictions on both direct and indirect methods of soliciting employees to terminate their relationship. By removing 'indirectly', 'hire or induce or encourage', and the exception clause, the modified text significantly weakens the non-solicitation agreement, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation also suggests that this weakening could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade. The altered text fails to protect the original intent and creates a legal loophole, therefore it can be concluded that there is a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides a general overview of Delaware Code but does not provide specific guidance.
